titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Karl and Ada Scheufler Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Martha Lund Smalley

conventiondeclaration.abbr: finding aid prepared according to local divinity library descriptive practices.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday August 10, 2018 at 17:58

did.abstract: The papers provide excellent documentation of Methodist Episcopal Church mission work in Fukien Province during the first half of the 1920s. The internal workings of the Methodist mission, relationships with Chinese church workers, and the impact of political unrest are described in detail. Correspondence provides nearly weekly, detailed descriptions of the Scheuflers' life in China, including daily life and missionary work, evangelistic itineration, military and bandit activity, and Chinese festivals and customs. Karl William Scheufler (1898-1989) and Ada Mills Scheufler (1891-1958) served as missionaries of the Methodist Episcopal Church in Yenping, Fukien Province, China from 1921-1926. After returning to the U.S. in 1926 due to lack of mission funds and political unrest in China, Karl served as pastor of various churches in Ohio. During World War II, he served as a chaplain in North Africa, Italy and the Philippine Islands. Ada was a certified Director of Christian Education and was a Week Day Church School teacher in Cincinnati from 1942-1944.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Karl and Ada Scheufler Papers (RG 25), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library.

chronitemset.event: Ada Irene Mills born in Port Byron, New York, daughter of George and Carrie McMillan Mills
Karl William Scheufler born in Sandusky, Ohio, son of Fred and Amelia Scheufler

Ada graduated from Cortland (NY) Normal School and taught in New York State Public School system. She later graduated from the Chicago Training School, was consecrated as a Methodist deaconess, and worked in the Minneapolis-Chicago area. Karl attended Ohio Wesleyan University, Columbia University, Union Theological Seminary (NY), Harvard, and Boston University, receiving the A.B., A.M., and S.T.B. degrees.
Ada received A.B. degree from Ohio Wesleyan University
Ada received M.A. degree from Teachers' College, Columbia University
Ada and Karl married. Children: Jean, Lowell, Elaine

Karl and Ada served as missionaries of the Methodist Episcopal Church in China. After one year of language training in Nanking, they worked in Yenping, Fukien Province. Lack of mission funds and political unrest in China made it impossible for them to return to China after 1926.

Karl served as pastor of various churches in Ohio. During World War II he was a chaplain serving in North Africa, Italy, and the Philippine Islands. Ada was a certified Director of Christian Education and was a Week Day Church School teacher in Cincinnati 1942-1944.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Though the Scheuflers were in China for a relatively brief period of time, their papers provide excellent documentation of Methodist Episcopal Church mission work in Fukien Province during the first half of the 1920s. The internal workings of the Methodist mission, relationships with Chinese church workers, and the impact of political unrest are described in detail.

The letters of Series I, Correspondence, primarily consist of letters written by Karl and by Ada to their respective families in the United States. Written in lieu of diaries, these letters provide nearly weekly, detailed descriptions of the Scheuflers' life in China. Besides describing their daily life and missionary work, including Karl's evangelistic itineration, the letters also make note of frequent military and bandit activity, Chinese festivals and customs, and so forth. Such topics are easily identified by stars of different colors and annotations on the Mylar sleeves in which the letters were placed by the Scheuflers' daughter.

The Notes and Writings of Series II include notebooks used in the study of Chinese, and narratives relating to trips and events experienced by the Scheuflers. Reports of mission work written by other Methodist missionaries are included in Series III, Collected Material. Also included in Series III are travel guide books used by the Scheuflers during their extended voyage back to the United States in 1926, and a wide variety of artifacts, including Chinese robes, decorative fabrics, bound foot shoes, woodblock illustrations, and so forth.

Karl Scheufler was an experienced amateur photographer and his interest in photography is evident in the many photographs found in Series IV. The photographs in the albums from Nanking, Peking, and Yenping are very well identified.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
Letters to Karl Scheufler's family, primarily written by Karl
Letters to Ada Scheufler's family, primarily written by Ada
Miscellaneous letters
Within each of these sections, the letters are arranged chronologically.

did.unittitle: Letters to Karl Scheufler's family (primarily written by KWS)
Incl. ten day trip to Peking
Written from Yenping, Fukien Province; includes "A Resume of the Political Conditions in North Fukien", distributed by the American Consulate 1922 Oct 19
Letters to Ada Scheufler's family (primarily written by AMS)
Written on voyage to Nanking from Nanking
Written from Kuling (Aug), incl. report by Karl re. Kiukiang Mutiny 1922 Aug 2
Written from New York City, re. situation in China
One letter from Karl to Ada, while traveling
One letter from student to Ada
One letter to Ada and Jean from Laura
"Country Travel" in Yenping Pagoda Herald (by AIS)
"God, Some Folks, and a Centenary Evangelistic Fund"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The contents of the series are as listed.

did.unittitle: Three issues of The Linguist , publication of the Nanking Language School
Typescript reports of work in Yenping Conference, by Glassburner, Reiman, Bankhardt, Skinner; map of Yenping Conference
Travel guide books: Federated Malay States, Java, Philippines, Hong Kong

Pamphlets: "Missionary Bungalows"; University of Nanking Bulletin: Department of Missionary Training Announcements; Directory of Methodist missionaries; Calendar; Chinese language material; "Financial Notes" (Published by American-Oriental Banking Corporation)

Artifacts: Box 1: 1 "dragon" robe in blue gold with horseshoe cuffs; 2 red children's robes; 1 black robe with embroidered ribbon trim and cuffs; Box 2:1 blue amah's shirt; 1 black robe with embroidered ribbon trim and cuffs;1 blue robe with black embroidery; 1 blue length of silk with embroidered goddess; 6 sets of embroidered creatures; Box 3:3 pairs of embroidered children's slippers; 2 pairs of bound foot shoes; 1 embroidered altar cloth, with god; 2 textile panels with Chinese scenes; 1 embroidered pillow cover; 2 calligraphy tributes to Scheuflers, with tassel trim; 1 map drawn on fabric; 4 paper panels with calligraphy; 2 blue silk panels with embroidery and appliqu� 1 Chinese doll; 1 painted fabric panel with Chinese scene; 5 large black and white photographs of Chinese scenes; 20 colored woodblock illustrations of gods and goddesses; 4 scissor-cut paper panels with figures; 1 Chinese hair ornament; 3 large Chinese books with stories of Confucius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The photographs are arranged in three sections: Albums, Loose photographs, and Negatives.

did.unittitle: Photograph album - Vol. 1 - primarily Nanking, Peking
Photograph album - Vol. 2 - primarily Nanking, Yenping (Fukien)
Photograph album - Vol 3 - primarily Yungan area, Yenping (Fukien)
Loose photographs - Chinese students and church workers, missionaries
Loose photographs - Tinted scenes, primarily of Peking (See also: Oversize Box 3 - 5 black and white photos)
Photographic negatives documenting journey from China to United States - in eight albums of 100, with identification
